source.safety: Global Peace Index (Institute for Economics and Peace) via Wikipedia, with World Bank homicide rate as fallback
source.healthcare: UHC Service Coverage Index (World Bank/WHO) - data.worldbank.org
source.unemployment: World Bank ILO Unemployment (latest available) - data.worldbank.org
source.hdi: UNDP Human Development Index via Our World in Data - ourworldindata.org
source.happiness: World Happiness Report via Our World in Data (latest available) - ourworldindata.org
source.internet: Speedtest Global Index via Wikipedia (latest available) - en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_countries_by_Internet_connection_speeds

portugal.us_comparison: approximately 34% cheaper than the United States
portugal.quick_facts_paragraph: Portugal sits at the western edge of continental Europe with a single land border shared with Spain, and an Atlantic coastline that shapes both its climate and its culture. For Americans, the practical basics land well: the currency is the Euro, the language is Portuguese (not as widely spoken in English as some Western European neighbors), and EU and Schengen membership means stable institutions and easy regional travel. With a Gini coefficient of 33.9, income inequality is relatively moderate by global standards, which tends to correlate with a more balanced cost of living across the country.

spain.us_comparison: approximately 27% cheaper than the United States
spain.quick_facts_paragraph: Spain's membership in the Schengen Area and the Eurozone removes two of the bigger logistical headaches Americans face when settling into continental Europe, no currency conversion, no border friction when traveling regionally. At nearly 50 million people spread across just over 500,000 square kilometers, it's a genuinely large country with real regional variation, and that linguistic diversity reflects it: Spanish is the national language, but Catalan, Basque, Galician, and Aranese all carry official or recognized status in their respective regions. A Gini coefficient of 33.4 puts income inequality closer to the Western European norm than to the more stratified economies some Americans expect to escape.

mexico.us_comparison: approximately 54% cheaper than the United States
mexico.quick_facts_paragraph: With over 134 million people spread across nearly two million square kilometers, Mexico is one of the largest and most populous countries in the Western Hemisphere. It sits in the G20 and OECD, which signals a degree of institutional maturity, but a Gini coefficient of 42.6 means income inequality is a genuine fact of daily life, and where you land in that spectrum shapes your experience considerably. Spanish is the official language, and outside of expat enclaves, functional fluency matters.

germany.us_comparison: approximately 17% cheaper than the United States
germany.quick_facts_paragraph: With nine land borders and a population pushing 84 million, Germany sits at the center of Europe in every meaningful sense. It's a full member of the EU, Schengen, NATO, the G7, and the G20, which matters practically for anyone relocating: banking, travel, and legal residency all operate within well-established frameworks. The Gini coefficient of 33.7 reflects relatively moderate income inequality by global standards, and daily life runs on the Euro with German as the sole official language, so language study isn't optional for anyone serious about integrating.

italy.us_comparison: approximately 23% cheaper than the United States
italy.quick_facts_paragraph: With nearly 59 million people spread across a peninsula that touches six other countries and opens onto the Mediterranean, Italy is one of Europe's most connected nations in both geography and institutional terms. It's a full member of the EU, NATO, Schengen, and the G7, so the bureaucratic framework for American expats is well-established, and the euro means no currency surprises when traveling regionally. Income inequality is moderate by global standards, a Gini of 34.3, though daily life varies enormously between the industrialized north and the slower-paced south.

greece.us_comparison: approximately 37% cheaper than the United States
greece.quick_facts_paragraph: Full Schengen and eurozone membership makes Greece one of the more structurally straightforward European moves for Americans, and the euro eliminates any currency complexity once you're settled. With just over 10.7 million people spread across a country that's largely coastline and islands, daily life varies enormously depending on whether you're in Athens or somewhere far more remote. Greek is the sole official language, and outside major cities and tourist centers, functional English is far from guaranteed, so the language barrier is real and worth factoring in early.

costa-rica.us_comparison: approximately 44% cheaper than the United States
costa-rica.quick_facts_paragraph: With two land borders and coastlines on both the Pacific and the Caribbean, Costa Rica packs a surprising amount of geographic variety into roughly 51,000 square kilometers. Spanish is the official language, and the local currency is the Costa Rican colón, so Americans will need to factor in a real language shift and exchange-rate awareness into daily life. The country is an OECD member, which signals a degree of institutional stability, but a Gini coefficient of 45.5 points to meaningful income inequality that shapes everything from housing costs to public services depending on where you settle.

panama.us_comparison: approximately 48% cheaper than the United States
panama.quick_facts_paragraph: Income inequality here is among the highest in Latin America, and that tension is worth understanding before committing to a move. Panama's Gini coefficient sits at 49.7, meaning the gap between the country's wealthy enclaves and its poorer communities is stark and visible in daily life. The practical upside for Americans is real, though: the U.S. dollar is official currency alongside the balboa, which eliminates exchange rate anxiety entirely and makes budgeting feel familiar from day one.

thailand.us_comparison: approximately 66% cheaper than the United States
thailand.quick_facts_paragraph: With nearly 66 million people spread across a country about twice the size of Colorado, Thailand offers a scale that surprises many first-timers. Daily life runs on the Thai baht, the official language is Thai (a tonal script that takes real commitment to learn), and traffic moves on the left. The Gini coefficient of 33.3 suggests more economic equality than many of its Southeast Asian neighbors, which is worth factoring into any honest cost-of-living calculation.

malaysia.us_comparison: approximately 65% cheaper than the United States
malaysia.quick_facts_paragraph: With a population pushing 35 million spread across a landmass roughly the size of New Mexico, Malaysia sits at a geographic crossroads in Southeast Asia, sharing land borders with three countries and fronting two separate seas. English is widely spoken alongside Malay, which takes some of the friction out of daily life that Americans typically face when relocating abroad. The ringgit offers considerable purchasing power for dollar-earners, though a Gini coefficient of 40.7 signals real income inequality beneath the country's modern surface.

indonesia.us_comparison: approximately 66% cheaper than the United States
indonesia.quick_facts_paragraph: With nearly 290 million people spread across an archipelago of thousands of islands, Indonesia is one of the largest countries on earth by both population and sheer geographic scale. The official language is Indonesian, the currency is the rupiah, and daily logistics like driving happen on the left side of the road. A Gini coefficient of 34.4 suggests moderate income inequality by global standards, and Indonesia's G20 membership signals a serious, growing economy worth understanding before making any relocation decision.

colombia.us_comparison: approximately 66% cheaper than the United States
colombia.quick_facts_paragraph: With a Gini coefficient of 54.4, Colombia carries one of the higher income inequality ratings among OECD members, and that gap shapes daily life in ways that statistics alone don't capture. Spanning over 1.1 million square kilometers with coastlines on both the Pacific and the Caribbean, it's a genuinely large, geographically varied country where Spanish is the only official language. Americans who don't speak it will feel that barrier quickly.

croatia.us_comparison: approximately 50% cheaper than the United States
croatia.quick_facts_paragraph: With a population under four million spread across a country that borders five neighbors and opens onto the Adriatic, Croatia packs a surprising amount of geographic variety into a relatively small space. It joined the EU, Schengen, and the Eurozone more recently than most Western European members, but that full integration now means no currency exchange hassles and passport-free travel across much of the continent. A Gini coefficient of 30.1 puts it among the more economically equal countries in Europe, which tends to translate into a less stratified feel in daily life than Americans might expect from a place with coastline this photogenic.

czech-republic.us_comparison: approximately 46% cheaper than the United States
czech-republic.quick_facts_paragraph: One of the most equal societies in the developed world, the Czech Republic posts a Gini coefficient of just 25.7, putting it among the lowest income inequality figures in the OECD. Landlocked in the heart of Central Europe with four land borders, it sits firmly inside the EU and Schengen zone, which matters practically for anyone considering regional travel or long-term residency rights. Daily life runs on the Czech koruna, not the euro, so the currency is a real adjustment, and while Czech is the official language, it's a genuinely difficult one for English speakers to pick up.

poland.us_comparison: approximately 57% cheaper than the United States
poland.quick_facts_paragraph: One of the more equal societies in Europe, Poland's Gini coefficient of 28.5 puts it well below the global average on income inequality, which matters more than most relocating Americans expect. The country sits inside the EU and Schengen zone, borders seven countries, and has a coastline to the north, giving it a geographic range that surprises people who picture it as purely landlocked Central Europe. The currency is the Polish złoty, not the euro, and daily life runs entirely in Polish, a language with a steep learning curve for English speakers.

hungary.us_comparison: approximately 52% cheaper than the United States
hungary.quick_facts_paragraph: Landlocked in the heart of Central Europe with seven countries on its borders, Hungary sits at a genuine crossroads in ways that shape daily life there. It's a full EU and Schengen member, which matters practically for anyone thinking about residency and travel across the continent. Income inequality is relatively low by global standards, a Gini of 30.6, but the Hungarian forint and a language that shares almost nothing with English mean the adjustment curve is real.

romania.us_comparison: approximately 62% cheaper than the United States
romania.quick_facts_paragraph: Romania joined the Schengen Area in 2024, making it one of the EU's newer full members in that regard, and its relatively low Gini coefficient of 29.8 puts it among the more economically equal societies on the continent. The country isn't landlocked, sharing five land borders while also touching the Black Sea, which gives it more geographic range than many Americans expect. Daily life runs in Romanian leu, and Romanian is the sole official language, so language investment matters here more than in some Western European destinations.

bulgaria.us_comparison: approximately 61% cheaper than the United States
bulgaria.quick_facts_paragraph: Bulgaria sits at a crossroads that most Western Europeans overlook, sharing land borders with five countries and opening onto the Black Sea to the east. It joined NATO and the EU years ago, and as of recently adopted the euro, which simplifies finances for Americans used to tracking exchange rates. The Gini coefficient of 39.5 signals meaningful income inequality by European standards, so daily costs and quality of life can vary sharply depending on where you land.

vietnam.us_comparison: approximately 71% cheaper than the United States
vietnam.quick_facts_paragraph: With more than 100 million people spread across a long, narrow country hugging the South China Sea, Vietnam is one of Southeast Asia's most densely populated and geographically distinct nations. The official language is Vietnamese, which presents a real learning curve for most Americans, and daily transactions run through the Vietnamese đồng, a currency where exchange rates can make costs feel almost abstract at first. The Gini coefficient of 36.1 suggests a more moderate income distribution than many of its regional neighbors, which tends to show up in the texture of everyday life.

japan.us_comparison: approximately 10% cheaper than the United States
japan.quick_facts_paragraph: With nearly 123 million people spread across an archipelago of islands, Japan is one of the most densely populated and logistically intricate countries an American could choose to relocate to. The language barrier is real and steep, Japanese being one of the harder languages for English speakers to acquire, and daily life from reading food labels to filing paperwork requires serious long-term commitment to learning it. The upside is a society with relatively modest income inequality for its size, a strong G7 economy, and the kind of infrastructure and public safety that makes urban living feel genuinely comfortable once a person finds their footing.

south-korea.us_comparison: approximately 24% cheaper than the United States
south-korea.quick_facts_paragraph: With just over 51 million people packed into a country roughly the size of Indiana, South Korea offers a density of urban life that takes some adjustment. The single land border, shared with North Korea, means the peninsula is effectively an island for practical travel purposes, making the South Korean won and the Korean language the two variables that will define daily life most immediately. Income inequality here is relatively moderate by global standards, a Gini of 32.9 puts it in reasonable company among its fellow OECD members, which matters for Americans trying to gauge whether the middle-class infrastructure of healthcare, transit, and services will actually be accessible.

australia.us_comparison: approximately 11% more expensive than the United States
australia.quick_facts_paragraph: With nearly 28 million people spread across a continent the size of the contiguous United States, Australia offers an English-speaking landing pad that removes one of the biggest friction points of moving abroad. The income inequality here sits at a relatively modest Gini of 33.8, suggesting a more evenly distributed economy than many Americans are used to back home. A few practical realities do require adjustment: traffic moves on the left, and the Australian dollar means keeping a close eye on exchange rates when managing finances across both countries.

new-zealand.us_comparison: approximately 2% cheaper than the United States
new-zealand.quick_facts_paragraph: With just under five million people spread across 270,000 square kilometers of island terrain, New Zealand punches well above its weight as a destination for Americans drawn to an English-speaking country with genuinely intact nature and a functioning democracy. It's an OECD member and part of the Commonwealth, which signals stable institutions and a reasonably well-developed economy, but the distance from the United States is real and the New Zealand dollar means your finances get a new set of variables. Left-side driving is a small but daily reminder that this isn't just America with better scenery.

canada.us_comparison: approximately 5% cheaper than the United States
canada.quick_facts_paragraph: With just over 41 million people spread across nearly 10 million square kilometers, Canada is simultaneously one of the world's most populous countries and one of its least dense. That geographic reality shapes everything about daily life, from the vast distances between major cities to the cultural differences between an English-speaking west and a significantly Francophone Quebec. A Gini coefficient of 31.5 puts it among the more economically equal developed nations, which tends to show up in healthcare access and public infrastructure in ways Americans often notice immediately.

ireland.us_comparison: approximately 11% more expensive than the United States
ireland.quick_facts_paragraph: With a population of just over 7 million spread across a relatively compact island, Ireland manages to feel both deeply connected to the English-speaking world and genuinely distinct from it. The country runs on euros, sits firmly inside the EU and Eurozone, and shares only a single land border, with Northern Ireland to the north. A Gini coefficient of 29 puts it among the more economically equal societies in Europe, which tends to surprise Americans accustomed to sharper wealth gaps, though the cost of living in Dublin can offset that picture considerably.

netherlands.us_comparison: approximately 9% cheaper than the United States
netherlands.quick_facts_paragraph: With a Gini coefficient of just 25.7, the Netherlands ranks among the most economically equal societies in the world, which tells you something real about daily life there. Roughly 18 million people share a country smaller than West Virginia, bordered by only Germany and Belgium, with the North Sea defining much of its western edge. Dutch is the language, the euro is the currency, and full membership in the EU, Schengen, and NATO means the infrastructure and institutional stability Americans often take for granted are firmly in place.

france.us_comparison: approximately 18% cheaper than the United States
france.quick_facts_paragraph: France sits at the geographic heart of Western Europe, sharing land borders with eight countries while also holding Atlantic and Mediterranean coastlines. The Euro makes finances straightforward for expats already familiar with European travel, and membership in the EU, Schengen zone, and NATO signals a stable, deeply integrated institutional environment. At a Gini coefficient of 31.8, income inequality is relatively low by global standards, though the non-negotiable French language requirement will be the real test for most Americans considering the move.

argentina.us_comparison: approximately 61% cheaper than the United States
argentina.quick_facts_paragraph: With nearly 47 million people spread across one of the largest countries in the Western Hemisphere, Argentina offers a scale that can be hard to wrap your head around. Spanish is the primary working language for daily life, and the Argentine peso's chronic instability is something any serious relocator needs to research before committing. A Gini coefficient of 42.4 signals meaningful income inequality, which shapes everything from neighborhood character in Buenos Aires to the cost of services that expats often rely on.

ecuador.us_comparison: approximately 55% cheaper than the United States
ecuador.quick_facts_paragraph: With just over 17 million people spread across a country slightly smaller than Nevada, Ecuador punches well above its weight for American expats considering a move. The most immediately practical detail: Ecuador runs on the U.S. dollar, which eliminates currency risk and simplifies budgeting in ways that few other countries can match. That said, a Gini coefficient of 45.9 signals meaningful income inequality, so the comfortable expat life many imagine exists alongside significant economic disparity among Ecuadoreans themselves.

morocco.us_comparison: approximately 57% cheaper than the United States
morocco.quick_facts_paragraph: Morocco sits on the northwestern corner of Africa, where the Atlantic and Mediterranean coasts converge and three land borders connect it to the wider continent. With a population of nearly 37 million and Arabic and Tamazight as its official languages, the country presents a real linguistic learning curve for most Americans. The Moroccan dirham, a non-freely-convertible currency, adds a layer of financial adjustment, and a Gini coefficient of 39.5 signals moderate income inequality, meaning the gap between daily life for different economic classes is noticeable and worth factoring into any relocation plan.

switzerland.us_comparison: approximately 33% more expensive than the United States
switzerland.quick_facts_paragraph: Landlocked and bordered by five countries, Switzerland packs a remarkable amount of complexity into just over 41,000 square kilometers. It's a Schengen member but not an EU member, which shapes everything from work permits to banking, and the country operates on the Swiss franc rather than the euro. Four official languages, French, Swiss German, Italian, and Romansh, mean daily life can feel quite different depending on which region you settle in, and income inequality here is relatively low, with a Gini coefficient of 33.8.

norway.us_comparison: approximately 16% more expensive than the United States
norway.quick_facts_paragraph: With a Gini coefficient of just 26.5, Norway ranks among the most economically equal societies on the planet, which matters when you're sizing up daily life rather than just scenery. The country stretches along a long North Atlantic coastline and shares land borders with only three neighbors, giving it a geographic distinctness that matches its cultural one. Norwegian is the working language here, with Bokmål the dominant written form, and daily transactions run on the krone rather than euros, so expats will need to adjust to both.

denmark.us_comparison: approximately 7% more expensive than the United States
denmark.quick_facts_paragraph: With just over 6 million people spread across a country smaller than Virginia, Denmark punches well above its weight in quality-of-life rankings, and the Gini coefficient of 29.9 reflects one of the more genuinely equal income distributions you'll find anywhere in the developed world. It sits inside the EU and Schengen zone, which matters for practicalities like travel and residency frameworks, but daily life runs on the Danish krone, not the euro. The real adjustment for most Americans is the language: Danish is the official tongue, and while English fluency among locals is high, building a real life here means the language barrier eventually becomes a real consideration.

sweden.us_comparison: approximately 2% more expensive than the United States
sweden.quick_facts_paragraph: With one of the lowest income inequality scores in the world, a Gini coefficient of just 29.3, Sweden signals something that a lot of countries only claim: that prosperity is broadly shared. The country is a full EU and Schengen member, which matters practically for anyone thinking about travel, residency, and banking across Europe. One thing to plan for is the currency, since Sweden kept the Swedish krona rather than adopting the euro, and the language barrier is real for daily life until Swedish clicks.

belgium.us_comparison: approximately 12% cheaper than the United States
belgium.quick_facts_paragraph: With a Gini coefficient of just 26.8, Belgium ranks among the more economically equal societies in Europe, which matters if daily life affordability and social cohesion are part of what's driving a move. It's a small country, under 31,000 square kilometers, but it packs in four land borders, a North Sea coastline, and three official languages: French, Dutch, and German. For Americans, that linguistic complexity is the real adjustment, since which language dominates depends heavily on which region you settle in.

austria.us_comparison: approximately 12% cheaper than the United States
austria.quick_facts_paragraph: Landlocked but far from isolated, Austria shares borders with eight countries, making it one of the most connected nations in central Europe for overland travel. It sits inside the EU, Schengen, and the Eurozone, so daily life runs on euros and border crossings within the region are frictionless. A Gini coefficient of 31.2 signals relatively low income inequality by global standards, and German is the official language, the one real learning curve most Americans will face settling in.

finland.us_comparison: approximately 2% cheaper than the United States
finland.quick_facts_paragraph: Among the most equal societies on the planet, Finland posts a Gini coefficient of 27.4, a number that puts it in genuinely rare company and signals something meaningful about daily life there. A Nordic EU and Schengen member using the euro, it shares land borders with just three countries across its 338,000-plus square kilometers of forests and coastline. The main practical hurdle for American arrivals is language: Finnish is notoriously difficult, and while Swedish holds co-official status, neither is English.

united-kingdom.us_comparison: approximately 4% cheaper than the United States
united-kingdom.quick_facts_paragraph: With nearly 67 million people spread across an island nation that shares just one land border, with Ireland, the United Kingdom offers Americans something rare among major European destinations: no language barrier at all. English is the only official language, and the pound sterling means financial life is straightforward to understand even if the exchange rate takes some adjustment. The Gini coefficient of 32.4 reflects moderate income inequality by global standards, and membership in NATO, the G7, and the OECD signals the kind of institutional stability that tends to matter when someone is weighing a long-term move.

singapore.us_comparison: approximately 26% cheaper than the United States
singapore.quick_facts_paragraph: With just over six million people packed into 710 square kilometers, Singapore is one of the densest places on earth, and daily life reflects that intensity in ways both impressive and occasionally claustrophobic. English is an official language, which removes one of the biggest friction points for Americans considering a move, but the city-state's strict social codes and limited physical space represent a real adjustment. For those who can adapt, it offers a level of order and infrastructure that few places anywhere can match.

united-arab-emirates.us_comparison: approximately 28% cheaper than the United States
united-arab-emirates.quick_facts_paragraph: With just over 11 million people spread across a country roughly the size of Maine, the UAE is one of the more striking relocation destinations in the Middle East, and the numbers tell an interesting story before you even land. A Gini coefficient of 26.4 puts income inequality well below that of the United States, which surprises most Americans who expect the opposite. Arabic is the official language, the dirham is the currency, and daily life here tends to reward patience with bureaucracy more than fluency in the local tongue.

qatar.us_comparison: approximately 27% cheaper than the United States
qatar.quick_facts_paragraph: With a population of just over 3.2 million packed into a small peninsula jutting into the Persian Gulf, Qatar is one of the most logistically compact countries in the Middle East. Arabic is the official language, and the Qatari riyal is the local currency, so Americans arrive facing a genuine language barrier and an unfamiliar monetary system. The Gini coefficient of 35.1 suggests moderate income inequality by global standards, though daily life here looks very different from what most Westerners picture, shaped heavily by the country's labor migration structure and cultural norms.

saudi-arabia.us_comparison: approximately 48% cheaper than the United States
saudi-arabia.quick_facts_paragraph: With a population of just under 34 million spread across more than 2 million square kilometers, Saudi Arabia is a country of striking scale and stark contrasts. Arabic is the official language and English proficiency varies considerably outside major urban centers, so the language barrier is a real factor in daily life. The country is a G20 member and the Saudi riyal is a stable, oil-backed currency, but Americans should go in clear-eyed: this is a conservative Islamic monarchy with legal and social norms that differ fundamentally from what most Westerners are accustomed to.

iceland.us_comparison: approximately 21% more expensive than the United States
iceland.quick_facts_paragraph: With a population barely large enough to fill a mid-sized American city, Iceland packs an outsized quality of life into its 103,000 square kilometers of volcanic coastline. Its Gini coefficient of 26.8 puts it among the world's most economically equal societies, which shows up in everyday life in ways that matter, from wages to public services. The trade-off is real, though: Icelandic is the official language, the króna is its own currency rather than the euro, and the sheer geographic remoteness of island life in the North Atlantic is something worth sitting with before committing.

india.us_comparison: approximately 74% cheaper than the United States
india.quick_facts_paragraph: With a population of nearly 1.4 billion, India is not a country that eases you in gently. It shares land borders with six nations, stretches across more than 3.2 million square kilometers, and conducts much of its official business in English, which lowers the language barrier considerably for American arrivals. The rupee is the currency, traffic moves on the left, and a Gini coefficient of 25.5 suggests income distribution that is, by global standards, relatively compressed.

philippines.us_comparison: approximately 61% cheaper than the United States
philippines.quick_facts_paragraph: With over 112 million people spread across an archipelago of more than 7,000 islands, the Philippines is one of Southeast Asia's most populous and geographically complex countries. English is an official language alongside Filipino, which removes one of the biggest barriers Americans typically face abroad, and the peso keeps daily costs accessible by U.S. standards. The Gini coefficient of 39.3 signals meaningful income inequality, so the quality of life an expat experiences can vary sharply depending on where they settle and how they engage with local economic realities.

china.us_comparison: approximately 39% cheaper than the United States
china.quick_facts_paragraph: China shares land borders with 16 countries, a geographic fact that hints at just how vast and varied this place really is. At over 1.4 billion people, it is the world's most populous nation, and daily life reflects that scale in ways that can feel overwhelming to newcomers. The official language is Chinese, which presents a steep learning curve for most Americans, and the yuan operates within a tightly managed financial system that differs meaningfully from what most Westerners are used to.

georgia.us_comparison: approximately 72% cheaper than the United States
georgia.quick_facts_paragraph: With a population of just under 3.7 million, Georgia is a small country by almost any measure, but it punches above its weight as a destination for Americans reassessing where to live. It borders four countries, touches the Black Sea, and sits at the crossroads of Eastern Europe and Western Asia, which makes it feel genuinely unlike anywhere else. The currency is the lari, Georgian is the official language with its own distinct alphabet, and a Gini coefficient of 33.9 suggests a relatively moderate income distribution compared to many of its regional neighbors.

serbia.us_comparison: approximately 60% cheaper than the United States
serbia.quick_facts_paragraph: With eight land borders and no coastline, Serbia sits at a genuine crossroads of the Balkans, sharing boundaries with Hungary, Romania, Bulgaria, North Macedonia, Kosovo, Croatia, Bosnia, and Montenegro. Belgrade has emerged as one of Eastern Europe's more affordable and culturally energetic capitals, drawing expats who want a European city feel without Western European price tags. At a Gini coefficient of 32.8, income inequality is relatively modest, and daily life runs on the Serbian dinar, meaning currency exchange is part of the practical reality for anyone arriving with dollars.

kenya.us_comparison: approximately 63% cheaper than the United States
kenya.quick_facts_paragraph: With more than 53 million people spread across a country larger than France, Kenya is one of East Africa's most populous and geographically diverse nations. It borders five countries, has coastline along the Indian Ocean, and operates in English alongside Swahili, which meaningfully lowers the language barrier for American arrivals compared to much of the continent. The currency is the Kenyan shilling, income inequality sits at a moderate 38.5 Gini coefficient, and daily life runs on left-side driving, a detail worth factoring in early.

peru.us_comparison: approximately 55% cheaper than the United States
peru.quick_facts_paragraph: With five land borders and a Pacific coastline stretching the length of the country, Peru sits at a genuine crossroads of South America in both geography and culture. Spanish is the dominant language for daily life, but Quechua and Aymara remain officially recognized and widely spoken, so the linguistic reality on the ground is more layered than most expats expect. A Gini coefficient of 40.1 points to meaningful income inequality, something worth factoring in alongside the relatively affordable cost of living that draws many Americans to Lima in the first place.

brazil.us_comparison: approximately 57% cheaper than the United States
brazil.quick_facts_paragraph: With a Gini coefficient of 50.3, Brazil ranks among the most unequal economies in the world, and that gap shapes daily life in ways that matter to anyone seriously considering a move. At over 213 million people spread across more than 8.5 million square kilometers, it is a country of genuine scale, touching ten other nations and offering everything from coastal cities to deep interior. Portuguese is the language of daily life, the Brazilian real is the currency, and arriving without solid language preparation will slow things down considerably.

chile.us_comparison: approximately 45% cheaper than the United States
chile.quick_facts_paragraph: With nearly 20 million people stretched across one of the longest and narrowest countries on earth, Chile is a genuinely unusual place to call home. It shares land borders with just three neighbors but runs the full length of South America's Pacific coast, meaning the geography alone shapes daily life in ways that don't have obvious parallels elsewhere. Spanish is the only official language, the Chilean peso is the local currency, and a Gini coefficient of 43 signals meaningful income inequality that prospective residents should factor into their expectations.

albania.us_comparison: approximately 59% cheaper than the United States
albania.quick_facts_paragraph: With a population of just over 2.4 million, Albania is one of Europe's smaller and less-talked-about countries, a NATO member on the Adriatic coast that tends to fly under the radar for American expats. Daily life runs on the Albanian lek, and the language barrier is real since Albanian is linguistically isolated and shares nothing obvious with English, Spanish, or French. The upside is a Gini coefficient of 29.4, which puts income inequality well below the U.S., suggesting a society where the economic gaps between neighbors are noticeably narrower.

sri-lanka.us_comparison: approximately 74% cheaper than the United States
sri-lanka.quick_facts_paragraph: With a population of just under 22 million spread across an island roughly the size of West Virginia, Sri Lanka sits in the Indian Ocean off the southern tip of India, and that geography shapes almost everything about daily life there. English carries surprising utility alongside the official languages of Sinhala and Tamil, though neither is optional to ignore if you're serious about integrating. The Sri Lankan rupee means your dollar stretches considerably, and at a Gini coefficient of 37.7, inequality exists but isn't at the extreme end of the global scale.

egypt.us_comparison: approximately 77% cheaper than the United States
egypt.government_type: Unitary semi-presidential republic under an authoritarian government
egypt.quick_facts_paragraph: With over 107 million people, Egypt is one of the most populous countries in Africa and the Middle East, and Cairo ranks among the largest cities on earth by any measure. Arabic is the official language, which presents a steeper learning curve for most American expats than Western Europe would, and daily transactions run on the Egyptian pound. The relatively low Gini coefficient of 28.5 suggests more even income distribution than many developing economies, though that number tells only part of the story in a country of this scale and complexity.

bahamas.quick_facts_paragraph: With just under 400,000 people spread across an archipelago of more than 700 islands, the Bahamas is genuinely small, and that intimacy shapes daily life in ways both appealing and limiting. English is the official language and the U.S. dollar circulates alongside the Bahamian dollar at a fixed one-to-one rate, which removes one of the biggest friction points for Americans settling abroad. The left-side driving and Commonwealth ties are reminders that this is a distinct country with its own rhythms, not simply a warm extension of Florida.

belize.quick_facts_paragraph: With fewer than 400,000 people spread across a country roughly the size of New Hampshire, Belize punches above its weight as a destination for Americans looking to simplify their lives. English is an official language, which removes one of the biggest friction points in any overseas move, and the Belize dollar has been pegged to the U.S. dollar for decades, making financial planning more predictable than in most of Latin America. The Caribbean coastline and two land borders, one with Mexico and one with Guatemala, give it a geography that feels genuinely distinct from its neighbors.

bolivia.quick_facts_paragraph: Landlocked and sharing borders with five countries, Bolivia is one of South America's largest nations by area yet remains among its least visited by prospective American expats. Spanish is the dominant working language, but Aymara, Quechua, and Guaraní all hold official status, reflecting an indigenous cultural presence that shapes daily life in ways that set Bolivia apart from its neighbors. A Gini coefficient of 40.9 points to meaningful income inequality, something worth weighing honestly against the generally low cost of living that draws budget-conscious movers.

cambodia.us_comparison: approximately 65% cheaper than the United States
cambodia.government_type: Unitary parliamentary constitutional elective monarchy under a hereditary dictatorship
cambodia.quick_facts_paragraph: With nearly 18 million people spread across a country roughly the size of Oklahoma, Cambodia sits at a crossroads of Southeast Asia, sharing land borders with Thailand, Laos, and Vietnam while also holding a coastline along the Gulf of Thailand. Daily life runs on a genuinely unusual monetary arrangement: the Cambodian riel exists alongside the US dollar, which circulates freely and is widely accepted, meaning American transplants will find their bank cards and dollar bills surprisingly practical from day one. Khmer is the language of daily life, though, and learning even basic phrases goes a long way in a country where English fluency outside tourist areas and Phnom Penh can be limited.

cyprus.us_comparison: approximately 29% cheaper than the United States
cyprus.quick_facts_paragraph: With under a million people spread across a Mediterranean island smaller than Connecticut, Cyprus punches well above its weight for expats drawn to warm climates and European stability. It uses the euro, sits inside the EU, and carries a relatively low Gini coefficient of 31.8, meaning wealth is distributed more evenly than in many Southern European neighbors. Greek is the dominant language in the south where most residents live, but driving on the left, a legacy of British colonial rule, catches many Americans off guard on day one.

dominican-republic.us_comparison: approximately 63% cheaper than the United States
dominican-republic.quick_facts_paragraph: With just over 11.5 million people spread across roughly 48,000 square kilometers, the Dominican Republic shares its island with Haiti and has no other land neighbors, which gives it a geographic distinctiveness that shapes daily life in ways both practical and cultural. Spanish is the official language, so Americans without it will face a real adjustment, and transactions run on the Dominican peso rather than dollars, though USD is widely recognized in tourist corridors. The Gini coefficient of 39 signals moderate inequality, neither as severe as some Latin American neighbors nor as flat as European destinations, and that economic texture shows up clearly in the gap between Santo Domingo's polished neighborhoods and its poorer outer districts.

el-salvador.quick_facts_paragraph: The one fact that immediately stands out for American expats is the currency: El Salvador runs entirely on the U.S. dollar, which eliminates exchange rate anxiety and simplifies budgeting from day one. The country is small, roughly the size of Massachusetts, with Pacific coastline and two land borders connecting it to Guatemala and Honduras. Spanish is the language of daily life here, so anyone without it will face a real adjustment, and a Gini coefficient of 39.8 signals meaningful income inequality that shapes everything from neighborhood choices to the cost of services.

estonia.us_comparison: approximately 35% cheaper than the United States
estonia.quick_facts_paragraph: With a population of just under 1.4 million people, Estonia is one of the smallest EU member states by headcount, yet it punches well above its weight institutionally, holding full membership in NATO, the Schengen Area, and the Eurozone. That means no border hassles across much of Europe and the euro in your pocket from day one. Income inequality is notably low for a post-Soviet country, with a Gini coefficient of 30.7, and Estonian, a notoriously difficult language for English speakers, will be the main barrier to daily life outside Tallinn's expat circles.

ghana.us_comparison: approximately 62% cheaper than the United States
ghana.quick_facts_paragraph: With a population of 35 million and English as its official language, Ghana removes one of the biggest friction points Americans face when relocating abroad. Accra anchors a coastal West African nation that shares land borders with three countries, and day-to-day transactions run on the Ghanaian cedi, meaning currency fluctuation is a real factor to monitor. A Gini coefficient of 43.5 signals meaningful income inequality, so the quality of daily life here depends considerably on economic circumstances.

honduras.quick_facts_paragraph: With nearly 9.6 million people spread across a country roughly the size of Ohio, Honduras sits at the crossroads of Central America, sharing land borders with Guatemala, El Salvador, and Nicaragua while also holding Caribbean and Pacific coastlines. Spanish is the official language, and English proficiency outside tourist and expat corridors is limited, so anyone serious about relocating should plan on learning it. The Gini coefficient of 45.7 signals meaningful income inequality, which shapes daily life in ways prospective residents should research carefully before committing.

jamaica.us_comparison: approximately 46% cheaper than the United States
jamaica.quick_facts_paragraph: With just under three million people on an island roughly the size of Connecticut, Jamaica offers something genuinely distinct for Americans considering a Caribbean relocation. English is the official language, which smooths the transition considerably, though Jamaican Patois is widely spoken in daily life and carries its own learning curve. The Jamaican dollar and left-side driving are practical adjustments to factor in, and a Gini coefficient of 39.9 signals moderate income inequality, real enough to shape daily life depending on where and how someone settles.

kazakhstan.government_type: Semi-presidential republic under an authoritarian government
kazakhstan.quick_facts_paragraph: With a Gini coefficient of 29.2, Kazakhstan has notably low income inequality by global standards, which tells a different story than many Americans might expect from a Central Asian nation. It's an enormous landlocked country, the ninth-largest in the world at 2.7 million square kilometers, sharing borders with five nations and sitting far from any coast. Daily life runs on the tenge, both Kazakh and Russian are spoken widely, and Astana serves as the capital of a place that rewards patience from anyone willing to look past its geographic isolation.

latvia.us_comparison: approximately 43% cheaper than the United States
latvia.quick_facts_paragraph: Latvia sits on the Baltic Sea with land borders touching four countries, which gives this small EU member a geographic centrality that its modest population of just under two million might not suggest. It uses the euro, sits inside the Schengen zone, and carries full NATO membership, meaning the institutional infrastructure for an American expat is solid. A Gini coefficient of 34 puts income inequality in a relatively moderate range, and Riga functions as the cultural and economic center of a country where Latvian is the official language, so serious language study is worth the investment.

lithuania.us_comparison: approximately 49% cheaper than the United States
lithuania.quick_facts_paragraph: Full EU and Schengen membership makes Lithuania one of the more practical entry points into European life for Americans, and the euro as the official currency removes one layer of financial friction from daily transactions. With just under three million people spread across a country roughly the size of West Virginia, it has a genuine small-nation feel without being geographically isolated, sharing land borders with four countries and a Baltic coastline. A Gini coefficient of 36 puts income inequality in a moderate range, neither as flat as Scandinavia nor as stark as some of its regional neighbors.

malta.us_comparison: approximately 32% cheaper than the United States
malta.quick_facts_paragraph: One of the smallest countries in the EU, Malta packs a surprising amount of practical appeal for Americans into its 316 square kilometers of Mediterranean island. English is an official language alongside Maltese, which removes the daily friction that derails many expats in Europe, and the Euro makes finances straightforward across the continent. The Gini coefficient of 31.8 signals a relatively equal income distribution, though Americans should know they'll be driving on the left.

montenegro.us_comparison: approximately 60% cheaper than the United States
montenegro.quick_facts_paragraph: With a population of just over 600,000 people spread across a country smaller than Connecticut, Montenegro punches well above its weight in terms of geographic variety, from Adriatic coastline to dramatic mountain terrain. It uses the euro despite not being an EU member, which simplifies finances for American expats, and NATO membership since 2017 adds a layer of geopolitical stability that some of its Balkan neighbors can't offer. The Gini coefficient of 34.3 suggests a relatively moderate income distribution by regional standards, though the economy remains small and wages low compared to Western Europe.

nepal.us_comparison: approximately 73% cheaper than the United States
nepal.quick_facts_paragraph: Landlocked between India and China, Nepal packs extraordinary geographic range into a country not much larger than Illinois. With a population of just over 31 million and a Gini coefficient of 30, it's notably more economically equal than many of its regional neighbors, though that equality reflects modest incomes across the board rather than widespread prosperity. Kathmandu is the cultural and administrative center, Nepali is the official language, and daily transactions run on the Nepalese rupee.

nicaragua.government_type: Unitary diarchic presidential socialist republic under an authoritarian dictatorship
nicaragua.quick_facts_paragraph: With a Gini coefficient of 46.2, Nicaragua carries meaningful income inequality, and that gap shapes daily life in ways Americans should understand before committing to a move. The country sits between just two land neighbors on the Central American isthmus, with coastlines on both the Pacific and Caribbean, giving it more geographic range than its size suggests. Spanish is the official language, and the local currency is the Nicaraguan córdoba, so financial and social integration will require some groundwork for those arriving without the language.

north-macedonia.us_comparison: approximately 51% cheaper than the United States
north-macedonia.quick_facts_paragraph: Landlocked and sharing borders with five countries, North Macedonia is one of the smaller and less-discussed relocation destinations in the Balkans, with a population of just under 1.9 million. A NATO member but outside the EU and Schengen zone, it runs on its own currency, the denar, and daily life unfolds primarily in Macedonian, which means the language barrier is real for most Americans arriving. The Gini coefficient of 33.5 puts income inequality at a relatively moderate level, which is a more encouraging sign than many of its regional neighbors can claim.

paraguay.quick_facts_paragraph: With just over 6.4 million people spread across a landlocked South American nation roughly the size of California, Paraguay is one of the region's more overlooked relocation options. Daily life runs on the Paraguayan guaraní, Spanish is widely spoken but Guaraní is co-official and deeply embedded in local culture, so language immersion here is a genuine two-for-one. A Gini coefficient of 44.2 signals meaningful income inequality, something worth factoring in alongside the country's reputation for low costs and its borders shared with Brazil, Argentina, and Bolivia.

rwanda.us_comparison: approximately 73% cheaper than the United States
rwanda.government_type: Unitary semi-presidential republic under an authoritarian dictatorship
rwanda.quick_facts_paragraph: With four land borders and no coastline, Rwanda packs 14 million people into an area roughly the size of Maryland. English and French are both official languages alongside Kinyarwanda, so the linguistic barrier is lower than in many African countries, though daily life outside Kigali still runs largely in Kinyarwanda. A Gini coefficient of 39.4 signals meaningful income inequality, something worth weighing seriously against the country's reputation for rapid development and order.

slovakia.us_comparison: approximately 41% cheaper than the United States
slovakia.quick_facts_paragraph: Among the most economically equal societies in Europe, Slovakia carries a Gini coefficient of just 23.8, which puts it well below most of its neighbors and far below the United States. Fully integrated into the EU, Schengen, and the Eurozone, it offers Americans the kind of institutional stability that makes long-term relocation genuinely practical, with the euro as currency and open borders across five adjoining countries. The main adjustment is linguistic: Slovak is the sole official language, and English fluency outside Bratislava can be limited.

slovenia.us_comparison: approximately 34% cheaper than the United States
slovenia.quick_facts_paragraph: Few countries in Europe combine full EU, Schengen, and eurozone membership with a Gini coefficient as low as 24.7, making Slovenia one of the most economically equal societies on the continent. Slightly smaller than New Jersey, this country of just over two million people sits at a crossroads of four national borders, with a small slice of Adriatic coastline to the southwest. Daily life runs on the euro, Slovenian is the official language, and Ljubljana serves as the compact, manageable capital for a nation that quietly punches above its weight on quality-of-life metrics.

south-africa.us_comparison: approximately 51% cheaper than the United States
south-africa.government_type: Unitary parliamentary republic with an executive presidency
south-africa.quick_facts_paragraph: A Gini coefficient of 54.1 makes South Africa one of the most economically unequal countries on earth, and that's the central fact any American should sit with before seriously considering a move here. The country is large, coastal, and genuinely diverse, with 11 official languages and a rand that stretches a dollar considerably, but the inequality isn't just a statistic. It shapes neighborhoods, infrastructure, and daily security calculations in ways that vary dramatically depending on where someone settles.

taiwan.us_comparison: approximately 48% cheaper than the United States
taiwan.quick_facts_paragraph: With just over 23 million people packed into an island roughly the size of Maryland, Taiwan punches well above its weight as a place to live. Taipei is a world-class capital with modern infrastructure, and daily life runs on the New Taiwan dollar in a Chinese-speaking environment, which means the language barrier is real for most Americans and worth factoring in seriously. The island's geopolitical situation, unresolved and unlikely to simplify soon, is the other variable that tends to give prospective movers pause.

turkey.us_comparison: approximately 72% cheaper than the United States
turkey.quick_facts_paragraph: With over 86 million people and land borders touching eight countries, Turkey sits at one of the world's most genuinely consequential crossroads, spanning Europe and Asia across more than 780,000 square kilometers. It's a NATO and G20 member, but not part of the EU or Schengen zone, which shapes everything from residency rules to the currency situation. The Turkish lira has been volatile in recent years, and a Gini coefficient of 43.7 signals meaningful income inequality, so daily life looks very different depending on where you land and what you earn.

uruguay.us_comparison: approximately 37% cheaper than the United States
uruguay.quick_facts_paragraph: With just over 3.4 million people spread across a country roughly the size of Washington state, Uruguay often surprises Americans with how uncrowded and livable it feels. It sits between Brazil and Argentina with Atlantic coastline to the east, and Spanish is the only language you'll need, which matters more than people expect once the novelty of expat life wears off. Its Gini coefficient of 40 puts income inequality in a moderate range, notably lower than much of Latin America, suggesting a more evenly distributed economy than its neighbors.

budget_data_source: WhereNext (getwherenext.com), CC BY 4.0, World Bank ICP + Eurostat
